content.text:
<timestamp>Monday, Apr 27, 2026, 11:03 AM (UTC-4)</timestamp>
<user_query>
I'm getting this error on build, let's come up with a plan to fix:

[vite:css] [postcss] tailwindcss: /Users/duhl/git/ui/packages/ui/dist/theme.css:1:1: Cannot apply unknown utility class `mt-2`
file: /Users/duhl/git/ui/packages/ui/dist/theme.css:1:0
</user_query>
Looking into the tailwindcss and theme.css build error the user is experiencing.

The error message points to line 1:1 in the dist theme file, but the actual problematic `@apply` directive with the unknown `mt-2` class is further down at line 267. This suggests the error is being reported at the file's entry point rather than where the issue actually occurs, which means Tailwind or PostCSS is failing to process the dist file because it contains utility classes that aren't being recognized in that context.

Now I need to trace where this dist/theme.css file is being imported and understand why Tailwind v4's utilities aren't available when it's being processed.

I'm seeing the issue now — the build script just copies the raw theme.css file without processing it, so when other apps import it, they're getting Tailwind directives like `@apply` that haven't been compiled. Let me look at how the individual app stylesheets are set up to understand where the compilation should actually happen.

The issue is that Tailwind v4 can't resolve the `mt-2` utility class when using `@apply` inside `@layer components` — the theme utilities aren't being recognized at that point in the processing. I'm thinking through what's causing this conflict in how the CSS is being compiled. The `@theme inline` block shouldn't remove default variables, so that's probably not it. The error location at line 1:0 is likely misleading since PostCSS reports errors there generically. I'm realizing this might be a Tailwind v4 limitation with `@apply` resolution when CSS files are imported from outside the source paths or from node_modules via workspace symlinks—Tailwind v4 has known issues resolving utilities in `@apply` directives under these conditions.

The solution in Tailwind v4 is to either inline the styles directly as plain CSS instead of using `@apply`, or add a `@reference` directive to make Tailwind resolve the utilities properly. Since the theme.css file is being imported into a context where Tailwind is already loaded, I need to figure out why the utilities still aren't resolving even though `@import "tailwindcss"` comes first in the styles.css file.

Let me check what changed in the theme.css file recently to see if that's what introduced this error.

Looking at the git diff for theme.css, I see it only adds the success and warning variables without touching the mt-2 styling, so the dropdown-menu component must have been there already. Now I need to check what changed in the org-next/package.json file since that's marked as modified.

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && git diff apps/org-next/package.json apps/org-next/src/styles.css 2>&1 | head -100

content.text: I'm checking what changed in the donor app's root file—it looks like the CSS import switched from globals to theme. But I need to verify whether the donor app actually has the Tailwind import it needs, and I should also double-check if the error might be coming from org-next instead. Let me look at the donor app's styling setup to understand what's happening.

I'm tracing through the donor app's CSS import order—the theme file comes first with Tailwind utilities like `@apply`, followed by the main styles file that imports Tailwind itself, which could be causing the cascade issue in v4 where Tailwind needs to be loaded before custom utilities can reference it. The dist version comes from a package import that resolves through the exports field. I'm seeing that multiple apps import `@workspace/ui/theme.css` in different ways—some via `@import` statements in CSS files alongside the tailwindcss import, but others like donor, builder, org, and storybook are importing it directly from TypeScript/TSX files, which means they're separate modules without access to the tailwindcss context. Let me trace through those TSX imports to understand what's causing the issue.

Let me check the running terminals and recent build context.

 I'm seeing that multiple apps are now importing a shared theme CSS file from a package alongside their local styles, but Tailwind v4 treats these as separate files and doesn't register the utilities properly. The solution is adding a `@reference` directive to the theme CSS file so Tailwind processes it correctly.

Looking at the package docs, `theme.css` is the recommended import over `globals.css`, and according to Tailwind's documentation, `@reference "tailwindcss"` tells Tailwind to load utilities for use with `@apply` without duplicating the CSS output. Since the theme file also contains `@theme` blocks and component layer definitions that produce actual output, I need to understand exactly how `@reference` interacts with those—it seems like it would only prevent duplication of utility output while still allowing the theme definitions and component classes to work. I'm considering whether inlining the CSS properties directly would sidestep the issue entirely, since library styles shouldn't rely on Tailwind being available at apply time. But the `@reference` directive is less invasive. I'm now wondering if this is specific to how certain apps import theme.css separately versus bundling it through styles.css, and whether Tailwind v4 processes CSS imports by inlining them before applying transformations.
Checking the storybook styles and comparing configuration patterns with org-next to understand how they're structured.

Let me verify with the Tailwind v4 documentation on the `@reference` directive.

 So the issue is clear now—the `theme.css` file is trying to use `@apply mt-2`, but Tailwind doesn't recognize `mt-2` as a valid utility class in this context, likely because the Tailwind configuration isn't being applied correctly to the distributed CSS file.

The root cause is that when `theme.css` is imported directly as a JavaScript module (rather than through a CSS `@import` statement), Tailwind processes it in isolation without access to the default utilities. This is happening in several apps—donor, builder, org, and storybook—that are importing the theme file separately.

The fix is to add `@reference "tailwindcss";` at the top of the theme file so Tailwind knows to include its utilities when processing that CSS module. Still verifying the `@reference` approach...

I think the solution is to add a Tailwind reference directive at the top of the theme CSS file so it can resolve utility classes like `mt-2` and `absolute`, while the custom theme tokens defined inline become available to the consumer app's bundle. But I'm realizing theme.css itself is defining those tokens, so I need to think through whether the reference is actually sufficient.

When Tailwind processes the file with `@reference "tailwindcss"`, it should walk through and include both the default utilities and the custom theme definitions from the `@theme inline` block, which would make utilities like `bg-sidebar-background` resolvable alongside the standard ones. That should handle both the custom and default `@apply` rules.

There's another approach though — replacing `@apply` entirely with raw CSS variables and standard properties instead.

Writing CSS properties...

This would sidestep the Tailwind dependency issue at build time, but it's a bigger refactor. I think the `@reference` approach is cleaner since it's the documented Tailwind v4 solution and requires minimal changes.

I should also note that since the export in package.json points to `dist/theme.css`, users need to rebuild the packages/ui after making changes to the source file for those changes to propagate. Given they're already building, the next build should pick up the `@reference` directive.

Now I'm wondering whether `@reference "tailwindcss"` will still respect the custom theme variables defined in the `@theme inline` block, or if the reference replaces the entire theme context. I'm not entirely certain how Tailwind processes this, so I might need to test or look for examples to confirm the approach works with custom theme values.

The real question is whether the team prefers importing theme.css from JavaScript or using CSS `@import` statements. Some apps are already using the JS import pattern while others use `@import`, so I should probably propose the JavaScript import approach since that seems to be the direction they're moving, but also mention the CSS-side alternative as a simpler option if they want to consolidate everything into one PostCSS pipeline.

input.description: When was donor root last changed

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && git log --oneline --all -10 -- packages/ui/dist/theme.css 2>/dev/null; echo ---; git log --oneline -10 packages/ui/package.json

content.text:
I have enough context to put together a fix plan.

 I've identified the issue—the theme CSS file is using Tailwind utilities like `mt-2`, `absolute`, and `w-72` inside `@layer components` blocks, which is causing the conflict with how the styles are being applied.

The problem is that when Tailwind v4 processes this file as a separate module (imported directly via JavaScript), it doesn't have access to the default utilities, so it throws an error. But when the file is imported within another CSS file using `@import`, it works fine because the import gets inlined before Tailwind processes everything.

The fix is straightforward: I need to add a `@reference "tailwindcss";` directive at the top of the theme CSS file so Tailwind can resolve those default utilities when processing the file in isolation, then rebuild the package to update the dist version.

I'm checking the CSS spec ordering rules — `@reference` is a Tailwind-specific directive that should go before other at-rules, so I'll place it as the very first line, ahead of the font import. Since Tailwind strips out `@reference` during processing, the resulting CSS won't have any ordering issues.

Now I'm structuring the solution: I'll outline the root cause, list which files need the `@reference` directive added, include the rebuild step, and then show how to verify it's working.

input.name: fix-theme-css-mt-2
input.overview: Fix the Tailwind v4 build error by adding an `@reference "tailwindcss"` directive at the top of the shared `theme.css` so default utilities (`mt-2`, `w-72`, etc.) used inside `@apply` resolve when the file is imported as a standalone Vite/PostCSS module.

todos.content: Add `@reference "tailwindcss";` as the first line of `packages/ui/src/styles/theme.css`.
Re-run the failing app build (and ideally `pnpm build`) to confirm the `Cannot apply unknown utility class mt-2` error is resolved.

content.text: I'll run the full build from the root since I'm not sure which app is causing the issue, and this will take a moment to complete.

input.description: Run full build to verify fix

content.text: All 10 build tasks succeeded. Let me also confirm the original `mt-2` error is gone:

input.command: pnpm build 2>&1 | rg -i "Cannot apply unknown utility|error|fail" | head -20 || echo "no errors"

content.text:
No `Cannot apply unknown utility` errors remain. The other matches are third-party `"use client"` warnings and Vite asset filename hashes — unrelated.

 The remaining warnings are just `"use client"` directives from third-party packages and Vite's hashed asset filenames, so the actual utility error is resolved. I'll mark this as complete.

Done. Summary of the fix:

- Added `@reference "tailwindcss";` as the first line of [packages/ui/src/styles/theme.css](packages/ui/src/styles/theme.css). This loads Tailwind v4's default utility/theme context for `@apply` resolution without duplicating CSS in output.
- Rebuilt the package via `pnpm --filter @workspace/ui build:css`, which regenerated `packages/ui/dist/theme.css` (the script `cp`s the source).
- Verified with a full `pnpm build`: 10/10 tasks succeeded, no `Cannot apply unknown utility class mt-2` error.

Custom tokens like `bg-sidebar-background` keep working because they are defined in the same file's `@theme inline { ... }` block, and `mt-2`/`w-72`/`absolute`/etc. now resolve via the `@reference`.
